elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we propose a second-order dynamical system with a smoothing effect for solving paramonotone variational inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-002: Under standard assumptions, we prove that the trajectories of this dynamical system converges to a solution of the variational inequality problem.
elements.sent-2411-003: A time discretization of this dynamical system provides an iterative inertial projection-type method.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our result generalizes and improves the existing results.
elements.sent-2411-005: Some numerical examples are given to confirm the theoretical results and illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-006: Let $\Omega\subset\R^d$ be a nonempty, closed, convex subset and let $U:\Omega\to\Omega$ be an operator.
elements.sent-2411-007: The variational inequality (VI) of the operator $U$ on $\Omega$ is \begin{equation}\label{proVI} \tag{VI($U,\Omega$)} \text{to find $x_\star\in\Omega$ such that $\inner{U(x_\star)}{a-x_\star}\geq 0\quad\forall a\in\Omega$.}\end{equation} The solution set of the problem above is denoted as $\tap$.
elements.sent-2411-008: This problem plays a central role in optimization and has been in-depth investigated in the literature \cite{Bao,CensorGibali,Cruz,Iiduka2,Iiduka1,BelloIusem,HaiOPTL,KhanhVuong,Kinderlehrer,MalitskySemenov,SolodovSvaiter .}
elements.sent-2411-009: When $U$ is Lipschitz continuous and satisfies some monotonicity condition, numerous algorithms have been introduced for solving \ref{proVI \cite{AnhVinhDOI,CensorGibali,Facchinei,Quoc1 However, if $U$ is not Lipschitz continuous, there are very few methods for solving this problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In this work, we consider problem \ref{proVI when $U$ is paramonotone but is not Lipschitz continuous.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Under such an assumption, Hai \cite{HaiDS proposed the first order dynamical system \begin{equation}\label{hai26} x'(t)=\delta(t)\left\{P_{\Omega}\left(x(t)-\frac{\alpha(t)}{\max\{1,\|U(x(t))\|\}}U(x(t))\right)-x(t)\right\}, x(t_0)=x_0\in\Omega, \end{equation} where $\alpha,\delta:[0,\infty)\to (0,\infty)$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Assuming $\int_0^\infty \alpha (t)dt =\infty $ and $\int_0^\infty \alpha(t)^2dt <\infty$, the author proved that $x(t)$ converges to a solution of \eqref{proVI .}
elements.sent-2411-013: When the operator $U$ is strongly pseudo-monotone and Lipschitz continuous, Vuong \cite{VuongSIAM proposes the second order dynamical system \begin{equation}\label{202402122110}  x''(t)+\alpha_1(t)x'(t)=\delta(t)\left\{P_{\Omega}\left(x(t)-\alpha_0U(x(t))\right)-x(t)\right\}, x(t_0)=x_0\in\calh,\quad x'(t_0)=x_1\in\calh, \end{equation} where $\alpha_0,\alpha_1,\delta:[0,\infty)\to (0,\infty)$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: A finite-difference scheme for \eqref{202402122110 with respect to the time variable $t$, gives rise to the following iterative scheme: \begin{equation} y_k=x_k+\theta(x_k-x_{k-1}), x_{k+1}=(1-\rho)y_k+\rho P_{\Omega}(y_k-\lambda Uy_k). \end{equation} The second order dynamical systems \eqref{202402122110 has a close connection with the heavy-ball method and numerical methods with inertial effect.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, it requires more strict conditions than \eqref{hai26 does.}
elements.sent-2411-016: As a natural extension, it is interesting to study possibility of applying the second order dynamical system to solve paramonotone variational inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-017: This is the main aim of our paper.
elements.sent-2411-018: To do this, we propose the following dynamical system \begin{equation}\label{hai27}  y(t)\triangleq P_\Omega\left(x(t)+\lambda(t)x'(t)-\frac{\alpha_0(t)}{\max\{1,\|U(x(t)+\lambda(t)x'(t))\|\}}U(x(t)+\lambda(t)x'(t))\right), x''(t)+\alpha_1(t)x'(t)=\delta(t)[y(t)-x(t)], \end{equation} where $\alpha_0,\alpha_1,\delta:[0,\infty)\to (0,\infty)$ and $\lambda:[0,\infty)\to [0,\infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-019: We not only improve \eqref{hai26 to second order setting, but also smooth it out.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Namely, motivated by the idea in \cite{Alecsa , we add a damping term $\lambda (t) x'(t)$ to the argument of the projection.}
elements.sent-2411-021: According to \cite{Alecsa , this term annihilates the oscillations for the errors $\|x(t)-x_\star\|$, where $x_\star$ is a solution of \eqref{proVI .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Under the assumption that $U$ is paramonotone and continuous, we prove the convergence of trajectories generated by \eqref{hai27 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: It is worth mentioning that, to obtain the convergence of \eqref{hai27 , we need to prove that trajectories of this dynamical system do not leave set $\Omega$, which is an interesting result itself.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Further, by discretizing the dynamical system \eqref{hai27 , we obtain a novel iterative algorithms with an inertial effect.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The convergence of this algorithm is established under the similar assumptions as that of \eqref{hai27 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: There is a growing interest in using ordinary differential equations (ODEs) to design algorithms for optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-027: The earliest appearance of ODEs in this research direction may be the continuous gradient method \begin{equation}z'(t)=-\nabla f(z(t))\end{equation} in connection with the unconstrained minimization problem of the differentiable convex function $f$.
elements.sent-2411-028: In order to enrich the methods solving, researchers have increased the order of ODEs with the desire that equations obtained exhibit a better performance.
elements.sent-2411-029: Polyak \cite{Polyak proposed the Heavy Ball with Friction method to accelerate the gradient descent method.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Polyak's method regards an inertial system with a fixed viscous damping coefficient \begin{equation}\label{HB} z''(t) +\delta z'(t)=-\nabla f (z(t)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-031: The system \eqref{HB was extended for constrained optimization as well as co-coercive operator (see \cite{AA ).}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Later, Bo\c t and Csetnek \cite{Bot introduced the second order dynamical system enhanced by the variable viscous damping coefficient \begin{equation}z''(t) +\delta(t)z'(t)=-\theta(t)A(z(t)),\end{equation} where the operator $A$ is co-coercive in a real Hilbert space.}
elements.sent-2411-033: One finding in \cite{Bot states that it was possible to prove a weak convergence to the zero of the operator $A$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: These works have motivated the study of dynamical systems for solving monotone inclusions and optimization problems (\cite{Alecsa, zbMATH06522738, ACR, zbMATH07194541 ).}
elements.sent-2411-035: The survey \cite{zbMATH07344788 is a good source providing the recent progresses in this research direction.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The remainder of this paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-037: Section 2 collects some concepts and results which will be frequently used in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-038: In Section 3, we prove the convergence of the dynamical system in continuous time.
elements.sent-2411-039: Section 4 is devoted to convergence of an inertial iterative projection-type algorithm, obtained via discretization of the corresponding dynamical system.
elements.sent-2411-040: Section \ref{sec-exam gives some numerical examples to confirm the theoretical results and illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Let $X_{\geq a}\triangleq\{t\in X:t\geq a\}$ and $X_{>a}\triangleq\{t\in X:t>a\}$, where $X\subseteq\R$.
elements.sent-2411-042: For $p\geq 1$, the space $L^p$ consists of functions that are $p$-power Lebesgue integrable over $\R_{\geq 0}$.
elements.sent-2411-043: The space $\ell^p$ is a type of sequence space that consist of sequences of numbers that are $p$-power summable over $\Z_{\geq 0}$.
elements.sent-2411-044: The space $\ell^\infty$ contains sequences of numbers that are bounded over $\Z_{\geq 0}$.
elements.sent-2411-045: For the purpose of the paper, it is to recall some terminologies.

elements.sent-2411-158: Condition \eqref{202402040925 reveals \begin{equation}\beta_1(n) \geq\xi(n)[\beta_1(n)-1]+\beta_1(n)[\beta_1(n)-1]+\xi(n)[\xi(n)+\beta_1(n)-1][\eta(n)^2+Q_1]+Q_2 \geq\xi(n)^2[\eta(n)^2+Q_1]+Q_2\quad(as $\beta_1(n)\geq 1$) >\xi(n)^2\eta(n)^2+Q_2,\end{equation} which implies, as $\beta_1(n)\geq 1$, that \begin{equation}\beta_1(n)^2>\xi(n)^2\eta(n)^2+Q_2.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-163: The inequality above is equivalent to the following \begin{equation}c^{\dla}(n)+[2\beta_1(n)-\xi(n)(\beta_1(n)-1)-\beta_1(n)^2]c(n) \leq\xi(n)[\xi(n)+\beta_1(n)-1]\cdot\|w(n)-z(n)\|^2 \leq\xi(n)[\xi(n)+\beta_1(n)-1]\cdot[-\eta(n)\|z^{\nabla}(n)\|+\beta_0(n)]^2\quad\text{(by \eqref{202402092025})}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-164: Using the the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, we get \begin{equation}c^{\dla}(n)+[2\beta_1(n)-\xi(n)(\beta_1(n)-1)-\beta_1(n)^2]c(n) \leq\xi(n)[\xi(n)+\beta_1(n)-1]\cdot[Q_1c(n)+\beta_0(n)^2]\left(\frac{\eta(n)^2}{Q_1}+1\right),\end{equation} which is equivalent to saying that \begin{equation}c^{\dla}(n)+[2\beta_1(n)-\xi(n)(\beta_1(n)-1)-\beta_1(n)^2-\xi(n)(\xi(n)+\beta_1(n)-1)(\eta(n)^2+Q_1)]c(n) \leq\xi(n)(\xi(n)+\beta_1(n)-1)\left(\frac{\eta(n)^2}{Q_1}+1\right)\beta_0(n)^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-165: By \eqref{202402040925 and \eqref{202402032026 , we estimate \begin{equation}c^{\dla}(n)+Q_2c(n) \leq 2\xi(n)\left(\frac{\eta(n)^2}{Q_1}+1\right)\beta_0(n)^2 \leq 2\left(\frac{1}{Q_1}+1\right)\xi(n)\beta_0(n)^2,\end{equation} where we use condition \eqref{202402092106 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-166: Summing the line above from $n=1$ to $n=m-1$ yields \begin{equation}c(m)-c(1)+Q_2\sum_{n=1}^{m-1}c(n)\leq 2\left(\frac{1}{Q_1}+1\right)\sum_{n=1}^{m-1}\xi(n)\beta_0(n)^2,\end{equation} which by \eqref{202402041047 , gives \eqref{2024020409376 .}}

elements.sent-2411-188: Let $p,q,\delta$, $\theta,$ $\lambda,\omega$ be the parameters satisfying conditions \eqref{202402131042 , \eqref{202402131043 , \eqref{202402131044 , \eqref{202402131045 , \eqref{202402131046 , \eqref{202402131047 , respectively.. \begin{equation}\label{202402131042}0<p<1, \label{202402131043}\frac{1}{2}(1-p)<q\leq 1-p, \label{202402131044}\delta>0, \label{202402131045}\theta>0, \label{202402131046}\lambda>0, \label{202402131047}\omega>\max\{e^{\frac{\ln(\delta+1)}{p}},e^{\frac{\ln\theta}{\lambda}}\}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Condition \eqref{202402040925 holds with the choice \begin{equation}Q_1\triangleq1-\left(\frac{\theta}{\omega^\lambda}\right)^2, Q_2\triangleq 1-\frac{\delta+1}{\omega^p}.\end{equation}. Indeed, \begin{equation}[\beta_1(n)-1+\xi(n)][\beta_1(n)-1+(\eta(n)^2+Q_1)\xi(n)] \leq\beta_1(n)-1+(\eta(n)^2+Q_1)\xi(n) =\frac{\delta}{(n+\omega)^p}+\frac{1}{(n+\omega)^p}\left(\frac{\theta^2}{(n+\omega)^{2\lambda}}+Q_1\right) \leq\frac{\delta}{\omega^p}+\frac{1}{\omega^p}\left(\frac{\theta^2}{\omega^{2\lambda}}+Q_1\right)=1-Q_2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-190: In this section, we present some examples to illustrate the effectiveness of our algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-191: These examples were tested using Matlab software, running on a PC with CPU i5 10400 and 16Gb RAM.
elements.sent-2411-192: Consider the problem \ref{proVI with \begin{equation}\Omega=\{ x\in \mathbb R^3: \|x\| \leq 1 \}\end{equation} and $U: \mathbb R^3 \to \mathbb R^3$, $U(x)=Ax$ for all $x \in \mathbb R^3$, where $ A= \begin{pmatrix 1 -2 1 3 1 3 1 -2 1 \end{pmatrix .}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: We applied Algorithm \eqref{202401310822 and \eqref{202402061434 to solve \ref{proVI .}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: In Algorithm \eqref{202401310822 , we choose the parameters as in Proposition \ref{pp1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-195: We fix $u=1, s=0.35, q=0.71$ and adjust the parameter $h$.
elements.sent-2411-197: The results are presented in Figure \ref{lientuc .}
elements.sent-2411-198: In Algorithm \eqref{202402061434 , we choose the parameters as in Proposition \ref{parameters with different $p, q, \delta,\theta, \lambda$.}}
elements.sent-2411-200: The results are presented in Figure \ref{viduroirac .}
elements.sent-2411-201: Next, we compare our algorithm \eqref{202402061434 with the Direct method (Dir.Method) (Algorithm 1 in \cite{BelloIusem ) \begin{equation}\label{DR} \begin{cases} x(0) \in \Omega, x(n+1)=P_\Omega\left( x(n) -\frac{\beta_0(n)}{\max\{1; \| U(x(n)) \| \}}U(x(n)) \right).\tag{Dir.Method} \end{cases}\end{equation} It was proven in \cite{BelloIusem that the trajectory generated by \eqref{DR converges to an element $z_\star\in\tap$ under the assumption that \begin{equation}\label{20241024} \sum\limits_{j=0}^\infty\beta_0(j)^2<\infty\quadand\quad\sum\limits_{j=0}^\infty\beta_0(j)=\infty.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Due to \eqref{20241024 , the Dir.Method uses the step sizes $ \beta_0(n)=\frac 1 {n^\tau $, with $\tau \in (0.5;1]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-203: In our algorithm, the step size is taken as $\beta_0(n)=\frac{1 {(n+\omega)^q}$, where the range of the exponent $q$ is wider than $\tau$ of the Dir.Method; in details, $q\in(0;1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-204: In the numerical examples, we fix $p=q=0.5$ and adjust the parameters $\delta, \theta, \lambda.$ Let $\omega =\max\{(\delta+1)^{1/p ,\theta^{1/\lambda}\}+1$.}
elements.sent-2411-205: The comparison are presented in Figure \ref{fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-206: As we can see, in all cases the new algorithm is more efficient than the existing one.
elements.sent-2411-207: We have proposed a second order dynamical system for solving paramonotone VIs in a finite-dimensional space and studied its global convergence.
elements.sent-2411-208: A discretization of this dynamical system gives rise to an inertial iterative projection-type algorithm for which we establish the convergence of the iterations.
elements.sent-2411-209: The topic for future research includes second order dynamical systems enhanced by the vanishing damping coefficient for solving paramonotone VIs.
elements.sent-2411-210: Data sets generated during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
